html:
PSE:DELM, Del Monte Pacific

The stale thesis is wrong in an important way: DELM is not a clean, debt-free post-restructuring stub. It is a highly levered Asian branded-food company whose U.S. disaster has been deconsolidated, but whose balance sheet still carries enough debt to make the equity unusually torqued. That distinction matters. The upside is not “normal staple rerating.” The upside is that a PHP 10.2 billion equity value is sitting under a Philippine operating business that is already earning at a run-rate far above the parent’s market cap.

The current price is PHP 5.25, the latest PSE close on May 15, 2026, with 1.944 billion shares outstanding and roughly PHP 10.2 billion of market value, or about $165 million at current USD/PHP rates. The stock is illiquid and ignored, but it is no longer a PHP 0.50 zombie stub. It has already recovered from the panic lows, yet screens still show ugly trailing losses, negative book value, no dividend, and bankruptcy headlines.

The core misunderstanding is the Del Monte brand mess. Retail discussion still collapses “Del Monte” into U.S. bankruptcy stories, California peach growers, the FY2025 audit disclaimer, and the PSE suspension. Those are real scars, but the listed equity now primarily lives or dies on the Asian business, especially Del Monte Philippines. The U.S. subsidiary filed Chapter 11, was deconsolidated effective May 1, 2025, and the bankruptcy court approved settlement and asset-sale orders in February 2026. DMPL has fully impaired $703.5 million of related U.S. assets and says the court approvals do not create a material change to the financial impact already recorded.

What remains is not mediocre. In 9M FY2026, continuing operations revenue rose 14.2% to $682.4 million, gross margin expanded from 27.9% to 33.2%, EBITDA rose 22.8% to $133.5 million, operating profit rose 39.7% to $116.6 million, and net profit from continuing operations reached $32.3 million. Del Monte Philippines itself is the jewel: 9M revenue of PHP 37.85 billion, EBIT of PHP 6.90 billion, and net profit of PHP 4.94 billion, up 41%. Annualized, DMPI alone is earning roughly PHP 6.5 billion, before considering that the public parent’s whole market cap is only about PHP 10.2 billion.

The reason that absurd comparison exists is leverage. At January 2026, group loans and borrowings were about $1.0 billion against only $9.9 million of cash. Total equity was negative $590 million, and net debt/adjusted EBITDA was still 5.9x. This is not a simple low P/E. It is a small equity slice on top of a large enterprise value. But that is also the torque: every $100 million of debt reduction is worth about 60% of the current market cap before any multiple expansion.

The forward setup is better than the backward-looking screens imply. Nine-month operating cash flow was $220.8 million. Capex was heavy at $131.3 million, leaving $89.4 million of free cash flow before interest payments. After roughly $50.6 million of interest paid, there is still meaningful cash generation. The company is also monetizing non-core Sundrop Brands shares in India, with the first 4.99% sale completed for about $15 million and further tranches contemplated. Management’s stated capital priority is raising equity or selling assets to reduce leverage and repair the capital deficit.

The business transition is also not just accounting. The U.S. dead weight is gone from consolidation, management is openly focused on the Asian core, and DMPI just installed Angie Go-Flaminiano as president and COO effective April 29, 2026. Her background at NutriAsia, P&G, and DMPI matters because DELM’s next phase is less about empire-building and more about brand execution, working-capital discipline, channel expansion, and deleveraging.

The best version of the thesis is this: the market is still pricing DELM as a governance-impaired, distressed holding company, while the operating reality is shifting toward a profitable Philippine and international pineapple/packaged-food platform with improving margins, strong category positions, and several non-equity ways to reduce leverage. The equity does not need a heroic consumer-staples multiple to work. If EBITDA moves from the current $165 million to $185-200 million, and net debt falls even modestly through free cash flow, Sundrop monetization, and refinancing, the current equity can double without the enterprise value looking stretched.

The main risk is not the brand. It is capital structure. Current borrowings remain large, covenant waivers matter, cash is thin, and a badly priced parent-level equity raise could cap the upside. The FY2025 audit disclaimer was tied to the U.S. restructuring, but it still damaged credibility. Governance is also not clean: controlling holders dominate, related-party transactions require monitoring, and the same broader control ecosystem oversaw the failed U.S. acquisition. This needs to be sized like a levered special situation, not like a defensive consumer staple.

My targets assume the current price of PHP 5.25.

Bear case: PHP 3.00, down 43%. Margins slip, refinancing remains difficult, fresh pineapple/export strength fades, and the company raises equity at the wrong level. The market keeps treating the stock as a distressed holdco option.

Base case: PHP 10.50, up 100%. FY2027 EBITDA reaches about $185 million, net debt falls toward $870 million, and the market values the business near 6.5x EBITDA. This is not an aggressive multiple; most of the upside comes from earnings normalization and debt shrinkage.

Bull case: PHP 22.00, up 319%. EBITDA reaches $210 million, the U.S. bankruptcy overhang fully clears, the company executes asset sales or a strategic capital raise without destroying minority value, and the market applies 8x EBITDA to a visibly repaired Asian branded-food platform. The stock becomes a multi-bagger because the equity is the thin part of the capital stack.
